poem.content:
The crowd swelled, a frantic, hungry sea,
because the taxi was late, you see.
Aunt Millie, bless her cotton socks,
was stuck downtown, amidst the ticking clocks.

“Hurry now! The order's placed, it’s main!”
she'd phoned, her voice a desperate, strained refrain.
What order? Some new scheme, some layered plan,
to make her fortune, as an adult can.

The viability of bacon, strangely clung
to market whispers, where the tune was sung
of profits promised, fortunes to be made,
a calculus of greed, a math cascade.

Any hope of sanity, seemed now to drain and seep,
a slow and silent plug, while shadows creep.
The engine stalled, beneath the sodium light,
a dead end road, in the encroaching night.
The plan collapsed, it was, by all rights,
not viable, as everyone could see.
Aunt Millie arrived, a little cross,
and gave a sigh, from all of this loss.
